verbatim_text: But in this work, you kind of treat the whole of Wikipedia as a single skill. So how do you avoid this correlation that Wikipedia then becomes nearly, I don't know, how you write encyclopedia-like particles rather than math skills and history skills? Well, yeah, I mean, yeah, that's a really, really good question. And it's very related to the skill discovery area. So in general, the granularity by which you define skills is very important, and it probably depends. How you do that is going to be very context dependent and depends on your application and so on. In this case, with avoiding having a skill to learn how to write Wikipedia articles rather than knowledge, you might want to break down your granularity to things that are more like sentence level or token level or something like that. Or maybe you only take the sentences or combinations of tokens from Wikipedia that are actually knowledge-based or something like that. Yes, how could you build the dependency run? So because now if you have like skill for token, then this would not be possible to now train so many networks, too. So in this case, you would probably need a different method for estimating the skills graph. So you have seen the commercial that says nine entities out of ten recommend X. So there were 11 experiments out of 12 where you were winning. But you didn't give any insight for what happened in the 12th and why things didn't work out there.
